definition: In the bias–variance decomposition, bias is the systematic error of a learning algorithm: how far the expected prediction E[ŷ] deviates from the true target, (E[ŷ] − y*)² at a point (or averaged over x). High bias means the model's assumptions are too restrictive to capture the real pattern — the underfitting tendency.
layman: Bias is the model being consistently wrong in the same direction because it is too simple or makes the wrong kind of guess. Like always predicting "mild weather" because your rule ignores season — you are systematically off, not just randomly noisy.
content:
## Bias in the bias–variance tradeoff

This topic is the **canonical home** for **bias** in the **bias–variance decomposition** — the part of prediction error that comes from **wrong or overly simple assumptions**, not from sensitivity to which training sample you happened to get (that is **Variance**).

> **Not this topic:** social or demographic **fairness bias** (e.g. model discrimination). That lives under ethics and fairness topics. Here, **bias** means **estimation bias** in statistical learning.

### Intuition

Imagine fitting a curve to noisy points:

- A **straight line** forced on clearly curved data will miss the bend **every time**, regardless of training set — that consistent miss is **high bias**.
- A **flexible curve** can track the shape on average — **lower bias**, though it may wiggle differently on each dataset (**variance**).

**High bias → underfitting:** training error and test error are both poor because the model cannot represent the true relationship.

### Formal view (squared error)

For a fixed input **x**, true response **y = f*(x) + ε**, and model prediction **ŷ = f̂(x)** trained on random sample **D**:

**Expected prediction error** decomposes (under standard assumptions) as:

**E[(y − ŷ)²] = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible error**

| Term | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| **Bias²** | **(E_D[ŷ] − f*(x))²** — systematic gap between average prediction and true function |
| **Variance** | **E_D[(ŷ − E_D[ŷ])²]** — spread of predictions across different training sets |
| **Irreducible error** | **Var(ε)** — noise in labels even with the perfect model |

**Bias** (not squared) at **x** is **E_D[ŷ] − f*(x)**. Squared bias is what enters the decomposition.

### What increases bias?

- **Too-simple hypothesis class** — linear model for nonlinear data, tiny network, shallow tree depth.
- **Strong regularization** — heavy L2/L1, early stopping before fit, high dropout.
- **Too few features** — omitting variables the target depends on.
- **Wrong inductive bias** — assuming linearity when interaction effects dominate.

### What decreases bias?

- **Richer models** — deeper trees, more parameters, polynomial or kernel features, larger networks.
- **Weaker regularization** (until variance dominates).
- **Better feature engineering** that exposes structure the model can use.

### Bias vs underfitting vs error on training set

| Signal | High bias (underfitting) | High variance (overfitting) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Training error | High | Often low |
| Validation error | High | High (gap from train) |
| Fix direction | More capacity, less restrictive model | Regularization, more data, simpler model |

Detailed underfitting/overfitting diagnosis lives in **Underfitting vs Overfitting**; here we own the **bias term** and its link to **systematic model misspecification**.

### Relationship to linear regression

Ordinary **linear regression** on nonlinear data often has **high bias**: E[ŷ] lies on the best-fit hyperplane, not on the true curved surface. Adding polynomial terms or switching model family reduces bias (see **Linear Regression** for the baseline linear form).

### Relationship to generalization

**Generalization** is performance on unseen data. **Bias** is one structural reason generalization fails even with infinite data: if the model class cannot represent **f***, extra data cannot remove systematic error — only **variance** shrinks with more data. See **Generalization** for the broader picture of train/test gaps and distribution shift.

### Key takeaway

**Bias measures whether your model is wrong on average because it is too simple or mis-specified.** Reducing bias usually means increasing model flexibility — traded against variance and compute.
keyPoints: Bias is systematic error: (E[ŷ] − f*(x))² in the standard squared-error decomposition.
High bias means the hypothesis class is too restrictive — the underfitting tendency.
Extra training data alone cannot fix high bias; you need a richer model or better features.
This topic is canonical for estimation bias in bias–variance; fairness bias is a different concept.
Lowering bias typically increases model capacity; variance and irreducible noise are separate terms.
